meta.name: Classic French Toast
meta.subtitle: Custardy inside • crisp edges • best with day-old sandwich bread or brioche

source.notes: Everyday French toast optimized for sliced sandwich bread (white or whole wheat). Includes dairy/non-dairy paths and oven-warm holding method.

yield.notes: Makes ~8 slices depending on bread size and thickness.

tasteProfile.notes: Warm cinnamon-vanilla aroma; balanced custard without being soggy.

ingredients.label: Sliced bread (day-old preferred; sandwich bread, brioche, or challah)
ingredients.notes: Thicker slices (3/4–1 inch) work best. If bread is very fresh/soft, dry it briefly (see notes).

ingredients.label: Milk (whole milk preferred; or half-and-half)
ingredients.notes: 1 cup. For richer custard, use half-and-half.

ingredients.label: Butter (for cooking)
ingredients.notes: About 2 tablespoons total; use as needed per batch.

ingredients.label: Neutral oil (optional, prevents butter from burning)
ingredients.notes: Use a small amount alongside butter, especially on very hot griddles.

steps.title: Optional: dry fresh bread for better soak
instructions: If bread is very fresh and squishy, lay slices on a rack and air-dry 10 minutes, OR toast lightly, OR warm in a 300°F (150°C) oven for 6–8 minutes to dry the surface.
Day-old bread usually needs no drying.

steps.title: Make the custard
instructions: In a shallow bowl or baking dish, whisk eggs until smooth.
Whisk in milk, sugar, vanilla, cinnamon, and salt until fully combined.

steps.title: Heat the pan
instructions: Heat a skillet or griddle over medium heat.
Add a small knob of butter (and a touch of oil if using). Pan should be hot enough that butter foams but doesn’t smoke heavily.

steps.title: Soak bread briefly (don’t saturate)
instructions: Dip each bread slice in the custard 5–10 seconds per side (thicker bread can go 10–15 seconds).
Lift and let excess drip off for a second so the surface isn’t dripping.

steps.title: Cook until golden
instructions: Place dipped slices on the hot pan. Cook 2–3 minutes per side until golden brown with crisp edges.
Work in batches; add more butter as needed between batches.
If browning too fast, reduce heat. If pale and drying out, increase heat slightly.

steps.title: Hold warm (optional) and serve
instructions: For batch cooking: hold finished slices on a rack over a sheet pan in a 200°F (95°C) oven until ready to serve.
Serve with maple syrup, powdered sugar, fruit, or butter.

variations.name: Extra-rich custard French toast
variations.change: Replace 120g of the milk with 120g half-and-half (or add 30g melted butter to custard).
variations.notes: More custardy center; great for brioche/challah.

variations.name: Cinnamon roll vibe
variations.change: Add 1g nutmeg and 2g extra cinnamon; serve with a simple glaze (powdered sugar + milk).

variations.name: Less sweet / more savory breakfast
variations.change: Reduce sugar to 10g and add a pinch more salt; top with butter and fruit (skip syrup).

variations.name: Dairy-free option
variations.change: Use unsweetened oat milk or almond milk and cook with oil instead of butter.

notes: Day-old bread gives the best texture (absorbs custard without collapsing).
If you get soggy French toast: reduce soaking time, increase pan heat slightly, and ensure pan is fully preheated.
If you get dry French toast: soak 2–3 seconds longer per side and cook slightly lower/longer.
For even cooking, use medium heat and don’t overcrowd the pan.

storage.fridge: Cool completely, store airtight up to 3 days.
storage.freezer: Freeze in a single layer then bag up to 2 months; reheat in toaster or oven.
storage.reheat: Toaster is best for crisp edges; oven at 350°F (175°C) for 6–10 minutes also works.

audit.testNotes: Schema-compliant: top-level schemaVersion+meta; ingredient objects include id+label and amount; steps include id+title and instructions.
